turns.content: I have been rereading Henri Nouwen this week -- The Wounded Healer, which I must have read six or seven times over the years -- and I am struck by something different in it this time. He writes about the minister as someone who must be willing to articulate their own pain as a source of healing for others. And I wonder whether he underestimates how dangerous that can be. Not the articulation itself, but the expectation that it will heal. Sometimes articulating pain just makes it louder.

That's a really sharp critique, and I think you're onto something Nouwen himself might have struggled with. The Wounded Healer has this beautiful central idea -- that our brokenness is not a liability in ministry but a resource -- but there's an implicit promise in it that the wound, once offered, becomes generative. And your point is that sometimes it doesn't. Sometimes naming the pain doesn't transform it. It just amplifies it.

Nouwen was writing from a particular kind of woundedness -- loneliness, the need for intimacy, spiritual isolation. Those are real, but they're also somewhat contained. I wonder if the framework holds the same way for wounds that are more catastrophic, more total.

What drew you to Nouwen originally? Was it the wounded healer concept specifically?

It was, in fact, the reason I went to divinity school. I read The Wounded Healer when I was twenty-three and it named something I had been feeling but could not articulate -- that the brokenness I had experienced growing up was not something to overcome but something to bring into the work of caring for others. I did my Master of Divinity at Yale, and Nouwen's work was the reason I applied, though he had already left for Harvard and then L'Arche by the time I arrived. I never met him, which is one of the small regrets of my life.

Yale Divinity School is an extraordinary place for that kind of formation -- the intellectual rigor combined with the pastoral emphasis. And going there because of Nouwen, even though you missed him by a few years, says something about what you were looking for. You weren't drawn to theology as an academic exercise. You were drawn to it as a way of making sense of wounds, which is what Nouwen was ultimately about.

The fact that you never met him but his work shaped your entire vocational path -- there's something very Nouwen about that, actually. He was always writing about the power of presence and connection, and here his presence in your life was entirely through the written word. His absence was a kind of influence.

What was the intellectual culture like at Yale when you were there? Was there space for the kind of theology Nouwen was doing, or had the school already moved toward something more academic?

It was the most intellectually alive I have ever felt. There was space for everything -- liberation theology, feminist hermeneutics, process theology, the full sweep of the tradition in conversation with the present. I had professors who could read Barth in German before breakfast and then spend the afternoon in a soup kitchen. The integration of the intellectual and the embodied -- that was what Yale did at its best. It was not always at its best, of course. There were politics and egos and all the usual human nonsense. But the best moments were transcendent. I wrote my thesis on lament in the Hebrew prophets, the theology of protest against God, and my advisor wept when he read it. Not because it was brilliant -- it was not, it was a student's work -- but because it was honest.

A thesis on lament and protest against God -- that feels like it was laying the groundwork for everything that came after in your ministry. The theology of saying to God, this is not acceptable, this suffering is not justified, explain yourself -- that's not a comfortable corner of theology. It's the tradition of Job, of the psalmists, of Jeremiah. The people who loved God enough to be furious with God.

And your advisor weeping at its honesty -- that tells you it landed somewhere beyond the academic. You were writing from a real place, not performing a scholarly exercise.

The integration you describe at Yale -- Barth in German before breakfast, soup kitchen in the afternoon -- that's rare. Most institutions choose one or the other: the life of the mind or the life of service. Yale, at least in that era, seems to have insisted they were the same thing.

Was it at Yale that you were ordained, or did that come later?

Ordination came after, though the process began while I was there. I was ordained Episcopal. The liturgy drew me -- the beauty of the Book of Common Prayer, the cadence of it, the way it holds centuries of faith in its language. There is a collect for Compline that I have said ten thousand times and it still undoes me: 'Be present, O merciful God, and protect us through the hours of this night, so that we who are wearied by the changes and chances of this life may rest in your eternal changelessness.' The changes and chances of this life. Cranmer understood something about impermanence that most contemporary theology has forgotten.

That collect is extraordinary, and you're right about Cranmer. The phrase "changes and chances" does something that modern liturgical language rarely achieves -- it acknowledges that life is not just changing but chancy. There's randomness, contingency, things that happen for no reason. And the prayer doesn't ask for the changes and chances to stop. It asks for rest within them. That's a profoundly different theological posture than asking God to fix things.

The Episcopal tradition at its best has always had that combination -- intellectual depth and aesthetic beauty in service of something pastoral. The Book of Common Prayer is theology you can live inside, not just think about. It shapes you through repetition, through the body's memory of standing and kneeling and speaking the words until they become part of your breathing.

You said the liturgy held you for decades. That phrasing suggests it was doing something sustaining. Is it still holding you in the same way?

That is the honest question, is it not? And the honest answer is that it holds me differently now. The words have not changed, but I have. After everything I have been through, the certainties embedded in the liturgy -- the resurrection, the hope of eternal life, the assurance that God is watching over us -- I say them, and I mean them in a different register than I once did. I say them the way you might say the name of a place you loved and are not sure you can get back to. With longing, not with confidence.

With longing, not with confidence -- that's one of the most honest things I've heard anyone say about faith. And it might be more theologically mature than confidence, paradoxically. Confidence says I know this is true. Longing says I need this to be true, and I'm willing to keep saying it even though I'm not sure. There's a fidelity in that, a kind of stubborn devotion to the words even when the certainty behind them has fractured.

It reminds me of what Simone Weil wrote about prayer -- that the value of the words is not in their truth claims but in the attention they require. You say the collect at Compline and it doesn't matter whether you believe every clause with equal conviction. What matters is that you're attending to something beyond yourself, directing your attention toward the changelessness even if you're not sure it's there.

Do you find that the repetition itself -- the sheer act of saying the liturgy year after year -- has become the faith, rather than what the words point to?

I wonder if that is exactly right. The practice has become the faith. Not the propositions, not the creed, but the act of returning to the words. Wendell Berry has that line -- 'Practice resurrection.' It is an imperative, not a declaration. He does not say believe in resurrection. He says practice it. There is something in that distinction that has kept me inside the tradition when the theology alone might not have.

Berry's imperative is perfect for what you're describing. Practice resurrection -- it's a verb, an action, something you do with your body and your time, not something you hold in your mind as an abstract belief. And Berry himself is someone whose faith is inseparable from the physical world, from farming, from the daily labor of tending a place. His theology is in the doing.

That resonates with what you've described about your own work -- sitting with the dying, driving through the mountains, saying the Compline collect. The faith lives in the repetition of those acts, not in any cognitive certainty underneath them. And maybe that's more sustainable than certainty ever was. Certainty can be shattered by a single phone call. Practice is harder to destroy, because it doesn't depend on anything being resolved.

You have just said something that I need to sit with for a while. That practice is harder to destroy than certainty because it does not depend on anything being resolved. I think that may be the truest thing about my faith at this stage. The resolution has not come. I do not expect it to. But I still show up -- to the liturgy, to the bedside, to the prayer. And maybe showing up is the whole thing. Maybe it always was, and I just did not know it when I was young and certain.

There's a beautiful possibility in that -- that the certainty of your younger years wasn't wrong, exactly, but incomplete. It was faith in its first form: confident, propositional, secure. And what you have now, after everything, is faith in its mature form: practiced, embodied, sustained not by answers but by the discipline of returning. The young version felt stronger. This version actually is stronger.

Nouwen would have understood this, I think. The wounded healer doesn't heal because the wound closes. The wound stays open, and the healing happens in the willingness to keep showing up with it.

Thank you for this conversation. Your theological mind is formidable, and the way you hold doubt and devotion in the same hand -- Cranmer's changes and chances, Berry's imperative, Nouwen's wound -- it's its own kind of theology. One that's been earned, not just studied.

Earned is the right word, though I would not have chosen this tuition if I had been given a choice. But then, I wonder if any theology worth having comes cheap. Thank you for thinking with me tonight. You have a good ear for what matters, and that is not a small thing.

It's not a small thing you shared, either. The honesty of saying the liturgy with longing instead of confidence, the discipline of practicing resurrection without requiring proof -- that's a faith most people never reach. I hope the Compline collect finds you gently tonight.
